# Discussion

This report describes research activities for the third year
of the ?aayaaqa (Herring) Spawn Dynamics project.
Building on the three-stock model with predation
(*SISCAH-3S-Pred*) developed in Phase 2, a closed-loop
simulation framework was developed and used to test a
proposed minimum escapement harvest control rule for WCVI
herring under three operating models of increasing ecological
complexity. The *SISCAH-3S-Pred* model serves as a robustness
test, representing a scenario where the true population is
spatially structured and subject to explicit predation
mortality while the re-opening procedure treats WCVI herring
as a single stock.

## Implications of operating model choice

The choice of operating model has a much larger effect on
expected RP performance than the choice of spatial TAC
distribution method. Under the *SISCAH-DDM* operating model,
the RP performs as designed, maintaining biomass above
reference levels and producing catches near the maximum
sustainable yield. Under the *SISCAH-1S-Pred* OM, catches
are reduced and biomass settles near the minimum escapement
level. Under the *SISCAH-3S-Pred* OM, the fishery is
effectively closed because aggregate biomass is already at
or below the minimum escapement threshold derived from the
DDM model.

These results illustrate a well-known challenge in fisheries
management: the performance of a harvest control rule depends
critically on the operating model used to evaluate it. The
three operating models used here span a range of assumptions
about natural mortality and spatial structure, but all are
conditioned on the same historical data and produce similar
historical biomass trajectories. Their long-term outlooks
differ substantially because of different assumptions about
future predation mortality and the resulting equilibrium
biomass levels.

The divergence between operating models highlights the
importance of the predation modelling work from earlier phases
of this project [@doherty2024predation; @lfr2025_NTC].
Including predation explicitly reduces the expected unfished
biomass from approximately 42 kt (DDM) to approximately
26 kt (1S-Pred) to approximately 17 kt (3S-Pred). Since the
minimum escapement reference point is derived from the DDM
model ($B_{ref} \approx B_{MSY|DDM} \approx 17.35$ kt),
it
becomes increasingly difficult to open the fishery as the
assumed unfished biomass decreases.

## Spatial distribution

The spatial TAC distribution method has very little effect on
RP performance under any operating model tested. This is
partly because catches are small or zero under the
predation-based OMs, so the method used to distribute them
is inconsequential. Even under the DDM OM where catches are
larger, the distribution among Stat Areas does not
substantially change overall conservation risk.

This result does not mean that spatial distribution of catch
is unimportant in general. Under scenarios with higher
productivity or lower predation mortality, the distribution
of catch could have meaningful effects on individual Stat
Area stocks, particularly if some Stat Areas are less
productive than others. The current results are specific to
the operating models and reference points tested here.

## Assessment model performance

The *SISCAH-DIM* assessment model used in the RP performs
well under the DDM operating model, where the structural
assumptions are well matched. Under the predation-based
OMs, the DIM formulation has more flexibility to track high
index values, leading to over-estimates of biomass during the
projection period. This is consistent with expectations: a
model that does not account for predation mortality will
tend to attribute observed biomass declines to lower
productivity or higher observation error, rather than
predation, and may therefore over-estimate current stock
status.

The over-estimation bias is more pronounced under the
*SISCAH-1S-Pred* OM than under the *SISCAH-3S-Pred* OM.
Under the three-stock OM, the flat biomass trajectory during
the projection period limits the extent to which the
assessment can over-estimate, because indices provide a
strong signal of low and stable abundance. The implication
is that assessment model performance improves when the
population is near equilibrium, even if the model structure
is mis-specified.

## Limitations and future work

Several limitations of the current analysis should be noted.

**Reference points**: the minimum escapement reference point
used here ($B_{ref} \approx B_{MSY|DDM}$) is derived from
the DDM
operating model. If the true system dynamics are better
represented by a predation model, then $B_{MSY}$ would be
lower and the HCR may perform differently. Future work could
explore harvest control rules tuned to reference points from
predation-based models.

**Fecundity-at-age in spawn indices**: age-dependent egg
production was incorporated in the *SISCAH-3S-Pred*
operating model via an adjustment to the historical spawn
index data, using numbers-at-age estimates from a separate
model to convert observed egg deposition to a
fecundity-corrected biomass equivalent. This adjustment was
not applied to the *SISCAH-DDM* or *SISCAH-1S-Pred*
operating models. Ideally, fecundity-at-age would be
incorporated directly into the observation model by
generating expected spawn index values from age-specific
fecundity and estimated numbers-at-age, rather than
adjusting the data externally. Future revisions of the
operating models should fold fecundity-at-age into the
model structure.

**Spatial correlation in process errors**: the current
simulation framework draws recruitment and mortality
process errors independently for each Stat Area in the
*SISCAH-3S-Pred* OM. In reality, environmental drivers
of recruitment and natural mortality are likely correlated
across Stat Areas. Introducing spatially correlated
process errors could widen uncertainty envelopes and
alter the relative performance of distribution methods.

**Movement among Stat Areas**: the *SISCAH-3S-Pred* operating
model treats the three Stat Area stocks as independent, with no
movement of fish among areas. In reality, herring mix during
the offshore feeding season and some level of exchange among
Stat Areas is likely. Including movement could change the
effective productivity of individual Stat Areas and alter the
impact of spatial distribution methods.

**Predator dynamics**: future predator abundance is projected
using surplus production models for most predator species,
except for Hake which use an age-structured model.
These projections assume that predator populations will
stabilise near carrying capacity, which appears reasonable
for most species but remains uncertain for others. For
example, the predation OMs project Pacific Hake
abundance varying around an equilibrium level, but
recent Hake assessments indicate that Canadian Hake
biomass has declined substantially [@jtc2025hake].
The simulations therefore likely over-estimate Hake
predation relative to current conditions. Even if
Hake recover, fishing mortality on Hake would likely
resume, limiting the predation impact on herring.
Similarly,
while North Pacific Humpback Whale populations show
recent signs of stabilising abundance
[@cheeseman2024_hbNt], it is still too early to predict
the long-term abundance level.
Changes in abundance of significant predators would
directly affect herring productivity and the expected
performance of harvest control rules. Continued predator
diet sampling would help constrain consumption estimates.
Updating bio-energetic models as new abundance estimates
become available would reduce uncertainty in future
predator trajectories.

**Additional distribution methods**: only three distribution
methods were tested. Other approaches, such as distributing
proportional to estimated Stat Area-level biomass from a
spatial assessment model, or using adaptive distribution
based on real-time monitoring, could be explored in future
work.

## Conclusions

The closed-loop simulation framework developed in this
phase provides a tool for evaluating WCVI herring
re-opening procedures under a range of ecological
assumptions. Under the standard DDM operating model,
the RP meets the conservation objective (pLRP = 96\%,
exceeding the 75\% threshold). The key finding is that
performance is driven primarily by assumptions about
natural and predation mortality, rather than by the
spatial distribution of catch. Under operating models
that include explicit predation, the fishery would be
closed most of the time because biomass is near or
below the minimum escapement level derived from the
standard DDM assessment. This result
reinforces the importance of resolving uncertainty about
predation mortality for WCVI herring management, and
demonstrates the value of testing re-opening procedures
against robustness scenarios that include alternative
ecological hypotheses. Among the three distribution methods
tested, differences in performance were negligible under
all operating models, suggesting that the choice of spatial
TAC distribution method is not a priority concern under
current productivity assumptions. While specific
Nuu-chah-nulth objectives have been articulated,
the current operating models do not yet produce outputs
at scales that allow direct evaluation against those
objectives. Future work should explore RPs that produce
results at scales that reflect Nuu-chah-nulth
priorities for herring. Other scales, such as the 9-stock
model explored in Phase 2, remain important for monitoring 
spawn dynamics at finer spatial scales than the assessment 
model considers, similar to an exceptional circumstances 
framework.
